Forecasting Men's Deadlifts in Powerlifting Competitions¶

2000-2019¶

What I Hypothesized and How I Was Thrown Off Course¶

I've been studying the women's powerlifting stats, somewhat selfishly. I decided for this assignment to show the menfolk some attention. There is also just more data on the men since there are more competitions and they have gone on for a longer time. So this time we have data from the mid 1960s, which is cool.

At first I was going to use Kalman, but the men's data turned out to be so quiet that I turned to using UnobservedComponents, which, I then realized is the Kalman Filter but with the tuning done for you. I also did not like the output. So I returned to Kalman afterall.

In [1]:
#Installation

#Libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN 
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose
import numpy.ma as ma
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.structural import UnobservedComponents
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_percentage_error
from pykalman import KalmanFilter
from statsmodels.tsa.regime_switching.markov_regression import MarkovRegression

#Isolate the men
df = df[df['Sex'] == 'M']

#Make a copy
raw_df = df.copy()

Formatting Year, Month, and Frequency for Modeling¶

In [5]:
#Create Dataframe with Best Monthly Deadlift with Exog Tested

# Convert Date to Datetime Format
df["Date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["Date"], errors="coerce")
df = df.dropna(subset=["Date"])

# Create Month + Year + YearMonth
df["Month"] = df["Date"].dt.to_period("M").dt.to_timestamp(how="start")
df["Year"] = df["Month"].dt.year
 

# Keep rows with target
df = df.dropna(subset=["Best3DeadliftKg"])

# Pick max deadlift row per month
idx = df.groupby("Month")["Best3DeadliftKg"].idxmax()

# One row per month, monthly frequency
df = (df.loc[idx, ["Month", "Year", "Best3DeadliftKg", "Tested"]]
        .set_index("Month")
        .sort_index())

df = df.rename(columns={"Best3DeadliftKg": "Deadlift"})
In [6]:
#Check frequency
print("freq:", df.index.freq)

freq: None
In [7]:
#Set again
df = df.asfreq("MS")
print("freq:", df.index.freq)

freq: <MonthBegin>

Initial Inspection¶

In [8]:
 #Initial Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
plt.plot(df["Deadlift"])
plt.title("Winning Deadlifts Over Time")
plt.show()

Observation: This is a much "tighter" plot than the women's deadlift. It has missingness early on. But it has much less variability than the women's as well as a trend that is steadily upwards but of lesser magnitude. I'm going to use only 1980 onwards since the data is very sporadic before then.

In [9]:
#Narrow the Temporal Scope

df = df.loc["1980":]

Exog Candidate: Drug Testing¶

Exog Candidates: What do I want to flag as a viable exog this time that is not so closely colinear? Candidates may be:

  • Tested vs not tested
  • Meet Country
  • Native Country of the Competitor
  • Equipment
  • The IPF Points which is a formula that adjusts for age and bodyweight

The two candidates that are most interesting to me is tested/not-tested and equipment. The relationship of testing to women's powerlifting surprised me with the highest weights occuring in drug-tested competitions. That said, there are also drugs that can be taken that, if stopped before competition, are not detectable. Drug testing and its initiation in the field is a story unto its own and I feel is a definite presence in the sport. I'm choosing testing as well because it is a disruptor, a shock if you will and unlike the others, is not directly related to brute strength.

Check Outliers¶

In [18]:
#I'm trying a different outlier detection method this assignment
#However in order to use DBSCAN I have to impute the  missing months discovered above

df_outliers = df.copy()

clustering = DBSCAN(eps=10, min_samples=5).fit(df_outliers[['Deadlift']])
df_outliers['dbscan_anomaly'] = clustering.labels_

# Plot results
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.plot(df_outliers['Year'], df_outliers['Deadlift'], label='Data')
plt.scatter(df_outliers['Year'][df_outliers['dbscan_anomaly'] == -1], df_outliers['Deadlift'][df_outliers['dbscan_anomaly'] == -1], color='red', label='Anomalies')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

Observation: There are far fewer outliers in the men's deadlift than in the women's. I'm not sure why this is. They the women's data would be so much noisier and abberant. But I feel comfortable eliminating these two extremes.

#I'm actually going to impute one outlier
df = df.drop(pd.Timestamp("1996-06-01"))

df = df.asfreq("MS")  # reintroduce the missing month as NaN
df["Year"] = df["Year"].interpolate(method="time")
df["Deadlift"] = df["Deadlift"].interpolate(method="time")
df["Tested_binary"] = df["Tested_binary"].interpolate(method="time")
df.isna().sum()

Data Preparation, Establishing Stationarity/Nonstationarity But Do Not Resolve¶

In [23]:
#Decomposition using the imputed series used in the outlier scan

decomposed = seasonal_decompose(df["Deadlift"], period=12, model='additive')
decomposed.plot()
plt.show()

Observation: This looks very seasonal like the women's. There is much less variance as noted before. The residuals are extremely close fitting. There is a clear upward trend. I'm beginning to wonder if this data is too well-behaved to be modeled by the Kalman Filter.

In [24]:
#ACF and PACF. ADF Test

# ACF and PACF plots
plot_acf(df['Deadlift'], lags=30)
plt.show()

plot_pacf(df['Deadlift'], lags=30)
plt.show()

adf_test = adfuller(df['Deadlift'])
print("ADF Statistic:", adf_test[0])
print("p-value:", adf_test[1])
print("Critical Values:", adf_test[4])
if adf_test[1] < 0.05:
    print("The data is stationary (p-value < 0.05).")
else:
    print("The data is not stationary (p-value >= 0.05).")

ADF Statistic: -1.229904116945492
p-value: 0.6606012765372553
Critical Values: {'1%': np.float64(-3.44464601455782), '5%': np.float64(-2.867843681679954), '10%': np.float64(-2.570127622873346)}
The data is not stationary (p-value >= 0.05).

Observation: The series is not stationary. The ACF shows clear autocorrelation above the confidence band with seasonal spikes and a cut off at lag 1. The PACf shows seasonal spikes. Cut off at lag 1.

Test/Train Split for Time Series¶

In [25]:
print("freq:", df.index.freq)

freq: <MonthBegin>
In [26]:
#Test/Train with Validation to Avoid Leakage While Tuning Hyperparameters
n = len(df)

train_end = int(0.8 * n)
val_end   = int(0.9 * n)

train_data = df['Deadlift'].iloc[:train_end]
val_data   = df['Deadlift'].iloc[train_end:val_end]
test_data  = df['Deadlift'].iloc[val_end:]

exog_train = df['Tested_binary'].iloc[:train_end]
exog_val   = df['Tested_binary'].iloc[train_end:val_end]
exog_test  = df['Tested_binary'].iloc[val_end:]

train_data = train_data.astype(float)  
print("NaNs:", np.isnan(train_data).sum())
print("Infs:", np.isinf(train_data).sum())
print(train_data.shape)
print(val_data.shape)
print(test_data.shape)
print("freq:", train_data.index.freq)

NaNs: 0
Infs: 0
(377,)
(47,)
(48,)
freq: <MonthBegin>

Model Fitting¶

I'm going to go with a Local Linear Trend with Seasonal because I tried Kalman Filter first and was encountering issues with is converging. Based on this, Chat, recommended Local Linear Trend because:

  • it handles a clear trend
  • seasonality
  • noise can be minimal
  • it handles missing values
  • it does well with structure with some noise
In [27]:
#Local Linear Trend # I found this online and it seems like it suited my needs

model = UnobservedComponents(
    train_data,
    level="local linear trend",   # upward trend
    seasonal=12                   # annual seasonality with monthly data
).fit(disp=False)

/opt/anaconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/statsmodels/tsa/filters/hp_filter.py:100: SparseEfficiencyWarning: spsolve requires A be CSC or CSR matrix format
  trend = spsolve(I+lamb*K.T.dot(K), x, use_umfpack=use_umfpack)

Generate the Forecast¶

In [28]:
#Set the steps
n_forecast = len(val_data) 

#Generate predictions
forecast_1 = model.get_forecast(steps=n_forecast).predicted_mean

Visualize the Forecast¶

In [29]:
#Training Data
plt.scatter(train_data.index, train_data, label="Observed", s=12, alpha=0.5)

# Latent Pattern / True State
plt.plot(train_data.index, model.level.smoothed, label="True State", color="blue")

# Forecast
plt.plot(val_data.index, forecast_1, label="Forecast", color="hotpink")

#Test Data
plt.scatter(val_data.index, val_data, label="Test Data", s=12, alpha=0.7, color='green')
Out[29]:
<matplotlib.collections.PathCollection at 0x3307fc550>

Observation: After checking my indexing and length of arrays over and over again, I've settled on the forecast looking comblike because it's capturing seasonality. The next step may be to remove seasonality from the model to see the underlying forecasted trend. I don't like this model. It doesn't really do anything for us in terms of clarifying the relationships. I do however like the hidden state line. I think that is helpful.

Extract Metrics¶

In [30]:
#Calculate Metrics
mape = mean_absolute_percentage_error(val_data, forecast_1) * 100
print(f"Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): {mape:.2f}%")

print("Observed mean:", train_data.mean())
print("Level mean:", model.level.smoothed.mean())
print("Level std:", np.std(model.level.smoothed_cov))
print("Seasonal mean:", model.seasonal.smoothed.mean())
print("Fitted mean:", model.fittedvalues.mean())

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): 4.38%
Observed mean: 373.28589794320993
Level mean: 373.27537885278457
Level std: 2.2185218854564472
Seasonal mean: 0.010105968020097406
Fitted mean: 372.65323885484264

Actual Kalman Filter with Baseline Transition Matrix¶

In [31]:
# Define state-space model with baseline transition matrix
#I'm starting with a baseline

transition_matrix = [
    [1, 0, 0],  # Trend component
    [0, 1, 0],  # Trend velocity
    [0, 0, 1],  # Seasonal component
]
observation_matrix = [[1, 0, 1]]  # Observations indicate trend and seasonality. 
H = observation_matrix

# Reinitialize Kalman Filter with seasonality
kf = KalmanFilter(
    transition_matrices=transition_matrix,
    observation_matrices=observation_matrix,
    initial_state_mean=[0, 0, 0],
    initial_state_covariance=np.eye(3),
    transition_covariance=np.diag([0.1, 0.1, 0.1]),  # Process noise
    observation_covariance=[[5]]  # Measurement noise
)


# Fit the Kalman Filter
kf = kf.em(train_data, n_iter=10)

# Apply the filter and smoother
filtered_state_mean, filtered_state_covariance = kf.filter(train_data)
smoothed_state_mean, smoothed_state_covariance = kf.smooth(train_data)#Hidden state

#Forecast
n_forecast = len(val_data)
forecast_mean, forecast_covariance = filtered_state_mean[-1], filtered_state_covariance[-1]
forecast = []

for _ in range(n_forecast):
    forecast_mean, forecast_covariance = kf.filter_update(
        forecast_mean, forecast_covariance, observation=None
    )
    forecast.append(forecast_mean[0] + forecast_mean[2])  # Combine trend and seasonality

forecast_index = range(len(val_data))
In [32]:
#Plotting Hidden State and Forecast

hidden2 = (H @ smoothed_state_mean.T).ravel()

plt.scatter(train_data.index, train_data, label="Observed", s=12, alpha=0.5)

# Latent Pattern / True State
plt.plot(train_data.index, hidden2, label="True State", color="blue")

# Forecast
plt.plot(val_data.index, forecast, label="Forecast", color="hotpink")

#Test Data
plt.scatter(val_data.index, val_data, label="Test Data", s=12, alpha=0.7, color='green')
Out[32]:
<matplotlib.collections.PathCollection at 0x3307456d0>
In [33]:
mape = mean_absolute_percentage_error(val_data, forecast) * 100
print(f"Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): {mape:.2f}%")

print("Observed mean:", train_data.mean())
print("Level mean:", hidden2.mean())
print("Level std:", np.std(hidden2))

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): 3.13%
Observed mean: 373.28589794320993
Level mean: 371.395936829988
Level std: 16.759908129408924

Observation: This is quite good. The forecast seems sensible and the hidden state reflects the shape of the data and its upward trend.

Remove Seasonality from Observation Matrix in the Kalman Filter¶

In [34]:
# Define state-space model with trend 
transition_matrix = [
    [1, 1],  # level_t+1 = level_t + slope_t
    [0, 1],  # slope_t+1 = slope_t
]
observation_matrix = [[1, 0]]  # Observations depend on trend. This is H

# Reinitialize Kalman Filter without
kf = KalmanFilter(
    transition_matrices=transition_matrix,
    observation_matrices=observation_matrix,
    initial_state_mean=[0, 0],
    initial_state_covariance=np.eye(2),
    transition_covariance=np.diag([0.1, 0.1]),  # Process noise
    observation_covariance=[[5]]  # Measurement noise
)

H = observation_matrix

# Fit the Kalman Filter
kf = kf.em(train_data, n_iter=10)

# Apply the filter and smoother
filtered_state_mean, filtered_state_covariance = kf.filter(train_data)
smoothed_state_mean2, smoothed_state_covariance = kf.smooth(train_data)


# Forecast
n_forecast = len(val_data)
F = transition_matrix
H = observation_matrix

state_mean = filtered_state_mean[-1].copy()
state_forecasts = []

for _ in range(n_forecast):
    state_mean = F @ state_mean
    state_forecasts.append(state_mean.copy())

state_forecasts = np.array(state_forecasts)          # (h, 2)
forecast3 = (H @ state_forecasts.T).ravel()
In [35]:
#Training Data

hidden3 = (H @ smoothed_state_mean2.T).ravel()

plt.scatter(train_data.index, train_data, label="Observed", s=12, alpha=0.5)

# Latent Pattern / True State
plt.plot(train_data.index, hidden3, label="True State", color="blue")

# Forecast
plt.plot(val_data.index, forecast3, label="Forecast", color="hotpink")

#Test Data
plt.scatter(val_data.index, val_data, label="Test Data", s=12, alpha=0.7, color='green')
Out[35]:
<matplotlib.collections.PathCollection at 0x330524910>
In [36]:
mape = mean_absolute_percentage_error(val_data, forecast3) * 100
print(f"Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): {mape:.2f}%")
print("Observed mean:", train_data.mean())
print("Level mean:", hidden3.mean())
print("Level std:", np.std(hidden3))

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): 14.56%
Observed mean: 373.28589794320993
Level mean: 371.5851230635573
Level std: 22.73499123469006

Observation: This shows that seasonality is really keeping the forecast undercontrol in the last iteration. Removing seasonality from the observation matrix has a definite negative affect on the MAPE and the appearance of the forecast.

Add Exog Variable (Tested Binary) to the Best Kalman Filter Iteration¶

Domain Context on Testing: Drug testing entered powerlifting competitions in the 1980s and 90s with greater penetration than it is bodybuilding, where performance enhancing drugs are still politely tolerated to this day. What I didn't know until I spoke to a female powerlifter at the gym was that testing happens AFTER the athlete has completed all lifts and is based on from what she can tell, who lifts the heaviest, who "looks like they are juicing" and a random selection that is done in the record keeping system.

So athletes may anticipate getting tested before they lift if they "look" a certain way vis a vis their competitors.

In [37]:
#I first have to calculate the coefficient of the Tested Binary
#Let's return to the easy version of Kalman Filter and get the coefficient for testing

results = UnobservedComponents(
    train_data,
    level="local linear trend",
    exog=exog_train
).fit(disp=False)

print(results.params)
train_data.shape

sigma2.irregular      504.763339
sigma2.level            0.123280
sigma2.trend            0.000065
beta.Tested_binary    -17.582298
dtype: float64

/opt/anaconda3/lib/python3.13/site-packages/statsmodels/tsa/filters/hp_filter.py:100: SparseEfficiencyWarning: spsolve requires A be CSC or CSR matrix format
  trend = spsolve(I+lamb*K.T.dot(K), x, use_umfpack=use_umfpack)
Out[37]:
(377,)

Observation: There is a significant negative relationship between the winning men's deadlift amount and drug testing. This shows that if you are tested at a competition, you will generally lift a lower winning weight. Perhaps the competitions that test aggressively and are "harder" in terms of their criteria for winning, will produce people who lift a bit lighter.

In [39]:
# Define Kalman Filter with Exog and Seasonal Component in the Transition Matrix 


beta = np.array([-17.582298])  # (1,)

#I need to turn the exog train and test into a 2 dimensional matrix with (,1) as the number of exogs
exog_train_2d = exog_train.to_numpy().reshape(-1, 1)
exog_val_2d = exog_val.to_numpy().reshape(-1, 1)


#This is how the exog enters the model by taking the coefficient and multiplying it by the exog train and test matrices
obs_offset_train = (exog_train_2d @ beta).reshape(-1, 1)  
obs_offset_test  = (exog_val_2d @ beta).reshape(-1, 1)  

transition_matrix = [
    [1, 0, 0],  # Trend component
    [0, 1, 0],  # Trend velocity
    [0, 0, 1],  # Seasonal component
]
observation_matrix = [[1, 0, 1]]  # Observations depend on trend and seasonality. This is H

# Reinitialize Kalman Filter 
kf = KalmanFilter(
    transition_matrices=transition_matrix,
    observation_matrices=observation_matrix,
    initial_state_mean=[0, 0, 0],
    observation_offsets=obs_offset_train,    #Exog
    initial_state_covariance=np.eye(3),
    transition_covariance=np.diag([0.1, 0.1, 0.1]),  # Process noise
    observation_covariance=[[5]]  # Measurement noise
)
H = observation_matrix


# Fit the Kalman Filter
kf = kf.em(train_data, n_iter=10)

# Apply the filter and smoother
filtered_state_mean, filtered_state_covariance = kf.filter(train_data)
smoothed_state_mean, smoothed_state_covariance = kf.smooth(train_data)

n_forecast = len(val_data)
state_mean = filtered_state_mean[-1].copy()

state_forecasts = []
for _ in range(n_forecast):
    state_mean = transition_matrix @ state_mean
    state_forecasts.append(state_mean.copy())

state_forecasts = np.array(state_forecasts)  # (h, 3)

y_forecast = (H @ state_forecasts.T).ravel() + obs_offset_test.ravel()
hidden4 = (H @ smoothed_state_mean.T).ravel()
In [40]:
mape = mean_absolute_percentage_error(val_data, y_forecast) * 100
print(f"Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): {mape:.2f}%")
print("Observed mean:", train_data.mean())
print("Level mean:", hidden4.mean())
print("Level std:", np.std(hidden4))

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): 3.66%
Observed mean: 373.28589794320993
Level mean: 380.42065047301276
Level std: 16.914794648361706

# Latent Pattern / True State
plt.plot(train_data.index, hidden4, label="True State", color="blue")

# Forecast
plt.plot(val_data.index, y_forecast, label="Forecast", color="hotpink")

#Test Data
plt.scatter(val_data.index, val_data, label="Test Data", s=12, alpha=0.7, color='green')

In [44]:
#Let's take a closeup 

plt.scatter(train_data.index, train_data, label="Observed", s=12, alpha=0.5)

# Latent Pattern / True State
plt.plot(train_data.index, hidden4, label="True State", color="blue")

# Forecast
plt.plot(val_data.index, y_forecast, label="Forecast", color="hotpink")

#Test Data
plt.scatter(val_data.index, val_data, label="Test Data", s=12, alpha=0.7, color='green')


plt.xlim(pd.Timestamp("2010-01-01"), None)
plt.legend()
plt.show()

Observation: The reason why the difference between tested and untested lifts is so constant is by virtue of the model itself adding the exog as additive and dictated by the -17 kg coefficient.

In [42]:
#Let's separate out the tested from untested athletes here in the training data

mask_train_1 = exog_train_2d[:, 0] == 1 #Shows the tested
mask_train_0 = exog_train_2d[:, 0] == 0 #Shows the untested

# Plot
plt.scatter(
    train_data.index[mask_train_1],
    train_data[mask_train_1],
    color="red",
    s=12,
    label="Tested"
)

plt.scatter(
    train_data.index[mask_train_0],
    train_data[mask_train_0],
    color="blue",
    s=12,
    label="Untested"
)

plt.legend()
plt.show()

Observation Looking at this might suggested the opposite: That testing decreases performance but this is just the raw data and not the model's estimation of the hidden state that is influenced by seasonality, shocks, noise, testing, and trend.

In [43]:
#Final Model Run


beta = np.array([-17.582298])  # (1,)

#I need to turn the exog train and test into a 2 dimensional matrix with (,1) as the number of exogs
exog_train_2d = exog_train.to_numpy().reshape(-1, 1)
exog_test_2d = exog_test.to_numpy().reshape(-1, 1)


#This is how the exog enters the model by taking the coefficient and multiplying it by the exog train and test matrices
obs_offset_train = (exog_train_2d @ beta).reshape(-1, 1)  
obs_offset_test  = (exog_test_2d  @ beta).reshape(-1, 1)  

transition_matrix = [
    [1, 0, 0],  # Trend component
    [0, 1, 0],  # Trend velocity
    [0, 0, 1],  # Seasonal component
]
observation_matrix = [[1, 0, 1]]  # Observations depend on trend and seasonality. This is H

# Reinitialize Kalman Filter 
kf = KalmanFilter(
    transition_matrices=transition_matrix,
    observation_matrices=observation_matrix,
    initial_state_mean=[0, 0, 0],
    observation_offsets=obs_offset_train,    #Exog
    initial_state_covariance=np.eye(3),
    transition_covariance=np.diag([0.1, 0.1, 0.1]),  # Process noise
    observation_covariance=[[5]]  # Measurement noise
)
H = observation_matrix


# Fit the Kalman Filter
kf = kf.em(train_data, n_iter=10)

# Apply the filter and smoother
filtered_state_mean, filtered_state_covariance = kf.filter(train_data)
smoothed_state_mean, smoothed_state_covariance = kf.smooth(train_data)

n_forecast = len(test_data)
state_mean = filtered_state_mean[-1].copy()

state_forecasts = []
for _ in range(n_forecast):
    state_mean = transition_matrix @ state_mean
    state_forecasts.append(state_mean.copy())

state_forecasts = np.array(state_forecasts)  # (h, 3)

y_forecast = (H @ state_forecasts.T).ravel() + obs_offset_test.ravel()
hidden4 = (H @ smoothed_state_mean.T).ravel()
In [44]:
#Final Model Testing with Test Data

mape = mean_absolute_percentage_error(test_data, y_forecast) * 100
print(f"Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): {mape:.2f}%")
print("Observed mean:", train_data.mean())
print("Level mean:", hidden4.mean())
print("Level std:", np.std(hidden4))


plt.scatter(train_data.index, train_data, label="Observed", s=12, alpha=0.5)

# Latent Pattern / True State
plt.plot(train_data.index, hidden4, label="True State", color="blue")

# Forecast
plt.plot(test_data.index, y_forecast, label="Forecast", color="hotpink")

#Test Data
plt.scatter(test_data.index, test_data, label="Test Data", s=12, alpha=0.7, color='green')

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): 3.29%
Observed mean: 373.28589794320993
Level mean: 380.42065047301276
Level std: 16.914794648361706
Out[44]:
<matplotlib.collections.PathCollection at 0x319519bd0>

Observation This captures an interesting upwardly shifted hidden state with a space where the validation data was. I'm not sure what the standard is in industry - do we fill this in when using test data? The foreacast captures the conservativeness of the second model while also adding flucuations associated with the binary exog.

In [49]:
models = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model Iterations' : ['Local Linear Trend', 'Kalman Baseline Transition with S and Trend Obs', 'Kalman No S but Trend and Level', 'Kalman Baseline with S and Exog', 'Kalman Baseline with S, Exog and Test Data'],
    'MAPE' : [4.38, 3.13, 14.56, 3.66, 3.29],
    'Observed Mean Kg': [373, 373, 373, 373, 373],
    'Level Mean Kg' : [373, 371, 371, 380, 380],
    'Level Std Kg': [2.2, 16.8, 22.73, 16.9, 16.9]
})

models
Out[49]:
Model Iterations MAPE Observed Mean Kg Level Mean Kg Level Std Kg
0 Local Linear Trend 4.38 373 373 2.20
1 Kalman Baseline Transition with S and Trend Obs 3.13 373 371 16.80
2 Kalman No S but Trend and Level 14.56 373 371 22.73
3 Kalman Baseline with S and Exog 3.66 373 380 16.90
4 Kalman Baseline with S, Exog and Test Data 3.29 373 380 16.90

Discussion¶

Metrics According to metrics, the best performing model for forecasting men's winning deadlifts is a Kalman Filter with an identity matrix as transition and seasonality and trend in the observation matrix. I wonder why the standard deviation increases so dramatically with using Kalman and not its "pre-packaged" version, Local Linear Trend. I would guess there is an internal mechanism that reduces variance. Or variance moved from slope to level.

Best Model I prefer model #4, a Kalman Filter with a baseline matrix for transition and seasonality and trend in the observation matrix as well as the exog of tested/not tested. You can see from Model 2 to 3, that seasonality is very important to capturing the data.

The Exog The introduction of the exog did not impact other metrics with the exception of increasing the level mean. So the times that athletes got tested, the hidden state in the data forecasts they will lift around 10 Kg more than they would if they are untested. This tracks with what I found out through conversation at the gym which is that testing is partly random but also targeted at those who break records, who perform the winning lift, and who "look" like they might be using peformance enhancing drugs.

What Story Does This Tell? Men's deadlifting has none of the noise and fluctuations that women's did. In speaking to lifters, they seemed to think this was because there are fewer women powerlifting. Sometimes you'll go to a competition and there won't even be another woman there so you wind up not being able to compete at all. Fewer people means more volatility. The men's deadlift is reliable and almost noiseless. The hidden state visualizes and captures some of the patterns that are not discernable from a simple plot of the raw data. Testing is linked to performance but for a variety of contextual reasons that were discussed. Stated succinctly, if you are tested in a powerlifting competition it's probably because you are attracting attention of some kind and you will already be lifting heavier. So the model captures more of a correlation than anything else. It wasn't a particularly effective exog, albeit it's interesting.

What I Would Do Next Time I think from what I can tell that this data might be better suited to the dynamics of a Hidden Markov model. I also think testing might have a nonlinear relationship to the winning lifts overtime. So perhaps a multiplicative model might tell us more. I had the urge to model tested and untested as two completely different hidden states that are also connected and for that I think I need to go to a different model.
